content:
{
  "title": "Semantic Sketch Prompting: Guiding Code Generation via Structured Problem Outlines",
  "problem": "Large language models often struggle with generating syntactically correct and semantically precise code, especially for multi-step or complex problems. Current prompting methods fail to provide enough structural guidance without verbose specifications.",
  "motivation": "Improving code generation is crucial as LLMs are increasingly used in software engineering. Prompting techniques that better capture the programmer's intent can reduce error rates and post-editing burden. With the growing size and capability of models, leveraging structured prompts is now more feasible and timely.",
  "method": "We propose Semantic Sketch Prompting, where the prompt includes a high-level, structured outline of the intended code, expressed as a sequence of semantic steps (e.g., input parsing, validation, core algorithm, output formatting), each with optional natural language annotations. The model is instructed to expand each step into code, maintaining explicit structure in the generation. A lightweight sketch-to-code adapter prompt is prepended to align model outputs with the semantic outline and reduce hallucination or omitted logic.",
  "experiment_plan": "Experiments will be conducted on HumanEval and MBPP, comparing Semantic Sketch Prompting to standard natural language prompts and chain-of-thought prompting. Metrics include functional correctness, syntactic validity, and alignment with the specified outline. Human evaluations will assess maintainability and faithfulness to the prompt structure."
}
